utilityName: City of Buckeye Environmental Compliance and Backflow Program
dueBasis: Buckeye says the city sends annual test due notices to backflow assembly users and requires annual testing at hazardous cross-connections under city code and ADEQ rules.
coveredDeviceTypes: Double check assemblies for engineered fire lines
Assemblies witnessed by the city inspector on development work
penalties: Buckeye treats annual testing as an owner responsibility and uses annual due notices to enforce compliance. Development-side testing must be witnessed and reported to the city inspector before occupancy.
sourceExcerpt: Buckeye is a strong Arizona utility because it pairs annual due notices with explicit assembly classes for irrigation, commercial sites, and engineered fire lines.
verdictSummary: Buckeye is a useful Arizona utility because it publishes annual due notices, clear required assembly classes, and direct owner responsibility for annual testing.
whoIsAffected: Buckeye customers with hazardous cross-connections, including multifamily, commercial, industrial, dedicated irrigation, and engineered fire-line services.
residentialNotes: Buckeye is strongest where a home has dedicated landscape irrigation or another hazard trigger rather than general domestic service.
Owner participation language makes the customer responsible for the annual test cycle.
commercialNotes: Commercial value is strong because Buckeye directly names multi-family, commercial, industrial, and irrigation facilities.
The city uses annual due notices rather than leaving compliance timing vague.

annualTesting.summary: Buckeye says owners must have annual tests and inspections performed on all required backflow prevention assemblies.
highlights: The city sends annual test due notices.
ADEQ-backed annual testing applies at hazardous cross-connections.
Owner expense and responsibility are explicit.
workflowSteps: Watch for the annual Buckeye due notice.
Hire a certified tester for the required assembly.
Keep the annual test current so the city program stays satisfied.

irrigation.summary: Buckeye is strong for irrigation because dedicated landscape irrigation systems require a reduced pressure principle assembly.
highlights: Dedicated landscape irrigation is named directly in city code language.
Annual testing still applies once the assembly is installed.
Buckeye uses the same hazardous-cross-connection logic as its commercial pages.
workflowSteps: Confirm the irrigation service is dedicated and protected.
Use the correct RP assembly for the irrigation line.
Complete annual testing on the city's due-notice cycle.

fireLine.summary: Buckeye explicitly carves out engineered fire lines as the place where a double check assembly is used instead of the default RP.
highlights: Engineered fire lines are named directly on the public page.
Fire-line assembly class is different from most other Buckeye hazards.
Development work also requires city-witnessed testing and reports.
workflowSteps: Confirm the service is an engineered fire line.
Install the correct fire-line assembly class.
Coordinate any development or inspector-witnessed testing with the city.
Confirm Buckeye has required an assembly at the service.
Use the right assembly class for the service type.
Complete the annual test when the city sends the due notice.
Keep any development-side report on file with the inspector if the site is still in buildout.
failureHighlights: Buckeye sends annual test due notices.
Dedicated irrigation and engineered fire lines have different assembly rules.
Development-side backflow tests can require city inspector involvement.

costBand.testingRange: Testing is market-priced, but Buckeye adds annual due-notice discipline and owner-responsibility pressure.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest costs rise on dedicated irrigation and engineered fire-line assemblies.
costBand.pricingNotes: The main local value is avoiding missed Buckeye due notices and failed occupancy/inspection handoffs.
